statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, move all zeros to the end of the array while maintaining the relative order of non-zero elements. The input array is guaranteed to contain only integers. You must modify the input array in-place and do not need to return anything. However, to assess the correctness of your solution, the output will be the modified array. For example, if the input array is `[0, 1, 0, 3, 12]`, the output should be `[1, 3, 12, 0, 0]`. If the input array is `[0, 0, 1]`, the output should be `[1, 0, 0]`. Your task is to write a function that accomplishes this.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, which denotes the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N space-separated integers, representing the array elements.
statement.outputFormat: The output will be a single line containing N space-separated integers, representing the modified array with all zeros moved to the end.
statement.notes: The input array will not be empty and will contain at least one element. The function should modify the input array in-place.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the array
Range of possible values for array elements

examples.explanation: The non-zero elements are moved to the front of the array while maintaining their relative order, and the zeros are moved to the end.
The single non-zero element is moved to the front, and the zeros are moved to the end.
Since there are no zeros in the array, the array remains unchanged.
Since all elements in the array are zeros, the array remains unchanged.

hints.text: Start by initializing two pointers, one at the beginning of the array and one at the end. The first pointer will track the position where the next non-zero element should be placed, and the second pointer will iterate through the array to find non-zero elements.

When the second pointer encounters a non-zero element, swap it with the element at the position pointed to by the first pointer. Then, increment both pointers. This way, all non-zero elements will be placed before the first pointer, and the first pointer will track the position where the next non-zero element should be placed.

Continue iterating through the array with the second pointer until it reaches the end. At this point, all non-zero elements will be placed before the first pointer, and the first pointer will be at the position where the last non-zero element was placed.

Now, all non-zero elements are in their correct positions, but the zeros are still in their original positions. To move the zeros to the end, we can simply iterate through the array from the beginning and swap any zero elements with the elements at the positions pointed to by the second pointer.

The key insight here is that we can use the two pointers technique to solve this problem in-place, without using any additional space. We can use the first pointer to track the position where the next non-zero element should be placed, and the second pointer to iterate through the array and find non-zero elements.

editorial.title: Move Zeros to End
editorial.intuition: The problem requires moving all zeros in the given array to the end while maintaining the relative order of non-zero elements. The brute force approach involves repeatedly finding and moving zeros to the end, but this is inefficient due to its high time complexity.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution is correct but slow because it involves iterating over the array multiple times, resulting in a time complexity of O(n^2), which is inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach is to use a two-pointer technique, but the given reference solution uses list comprehension to filter out zeros and then append them, achieving the same result more concisely.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution, as shown in the reference code, uses list comprehension to create a new list with non-zero elements followed by zeros, achieving a time complexity of O(n) and space complexity of O(n) due to the creation of a new list.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that it maintains the relative order of non-zero elements and moves all zeros to the end, as required by the problem statement.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution with a sample input, such as [0, 1, 0, 3, 12], would result in [1, 3, 12, 0, 0], demonstrating its correctness.
commonMistakes: Not maintaining the relative order of non-zero elements
Failing to move all zeros to the end
Using an inefficient algorithm with high time complexity
Not handling edge cases, such as an empty input array
Modifying the input array in-place instead of creating a new one
